feed.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary:
The recent communication highlights significant advancements and proposed optimizations in the use of cryptographic proofs within Bitcoin transactions, specifically leveraging STARK technology. A key suggestion involves simplifying the circuit used for generating these proofs by focusing on proving the knowledge of an extended private key (xpriv) that derives an extended public key (xpub) through hardened steps, rather than proving the derivation of a taproot output key from a BIP39 seed. This adjustment could potentially reduce the computational burden and enhance performance by minimizing the number of cryptographic operations required—from multiple HMAC-SHA512 calls and SHA256 compressions to just a single HMAC-SHA512 call and one point multiplication.
Further elaborated is the practical application of this streamlined approach. The prover would initially compute the xpriv outside the circuit and input it into the guest program, which then calculates and outputs the corresponding xpub. Verification of this proof involves checking the derived xpub against additional key-path elements provided by the prover, thus confirming the derivation without needing to perform complex taproot key tweaking inside the circuit. This not only makes the circuit more efficient but also extends its applicability to other types of cryptographic keys and addresses such as BIP340 xonly keys or various P2SH and P2PKH addresses.
The discussion also touches upon the implications of publishing xpubs and how, under certain conditions, it might still be safe from quantum attacks, primarily aimed at surveillance rather than direct forgery. Additionally, there's a suggestion to enhance scaling efficiency by using a single STARK proof to authorize transactions from multiple UTXOs within the same account, thus significantly reducing the verification workload.
Lastly, the email mentions an alternative commit/reveal scheme discussed previously, which, while potentially offering a lighter cryptographic load, demands careful design and possibly the inclusion of external quantum-safe mechanisms for initial commitments. The sender queries the recipient's opinion on choosing between commit/reveal strategies and STARKs for securing Bitcoin transactions against quantum threats.
This conversation reflects ongoing efforts and debates within the Bitcoin development community regarding the best approaches to enhancing transaction security and efficiency in the face of evolving technological challenges.
